summary: Gruber analyzes Microsoft's Windows Phone 7 announcement, offering observations on its UI, naming, competitive positioning, and strategic missteps. He credits Microsoft for finally starting over with a new platform after three years of ignoring the iPhone's disruption, but questions why they based it on the unsuccessful Zune UI and kept the 'Windows' branding. He identifies the Osborne Effect as a near-term threat, since current Windows Mobile phones are dead-ended. He argues Windows Phone 7's real competition is Android, not iPhone, because they share the same OEM licensing model — and Microsoft must justify licensing fees against Google's free offering.
key_points: Microsoft deserves credit for starting fresh, but waited three years after the iPhone to stop iterating on a failed platform
The 'Windows Phone 7 Series' name undermines the clean-break message by implying continuity with the unloved Windows Mobile line
The iPod Touch has no rival, making it the iPhone OS platform's single greatest competitive strength that Microsoft's phone-only branding cannot address
Current Windows Mobile phones face the Osborne Effect: announced obsolescence with no upgrade path will tank sales before WP7 even ships
Windows Phone 7's true competitor is Android, not iPhone, because both license to OEM hardware makers — but Microsoft must justify fees against Google's free model
money_quotes: Microsoft found themselves in a hole the day Apple unveiled the iPhone, but continued digging for three more years.
Basing the new Windows Phone 7 UI on the Zune raises the question of why they think it's going to fare any better than, well, the Zune.
I think the iPod Touch is the single greatest strength of the iPhone OS platform.
I think it shows just how perverse Microsoft's obsession with 'Windows' is.
The whole point of the 'Windows' name is that it was for a system whose UI revolved around the concept of on-screen windows. There are no windows in the Windows Phone 7 interface.
The 7 implies 'new version of the old thing', which isn't what they want at all because the old thing is unloved and unpopular.
Windows Phone 7 doesn't just have to be better than Android, it has to be better enough to convince handset makers that it's worth the licensing fees.
key_insight: Microsoft's Windows Phone 7 makes the right strategic move by starting over, but undermines its own clean-break narrative with legacy branding and faces a brutal competitive dynamic where it must beat a free rival enough to justify licensing fees.
